spec.objective: Implement the Container With Most Water algorithm to find the maximum area that can be formed between two vertical lines, using a two-pointer technique with O(n) time complexity and O(1) space complexity.
constraints: Must use two-pointer technique starting from both ends of the array
Must achieve O(n) time complexity (single pass)
Must achieve O(1) space complexity (no extra data structures)
Must handle edge cases: empty array, single element, two elements, all same heights, strictly increasing/decreasing
acceptance_criteria: Returns correct maximum area for [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7] which should be 49
Returns 0 for arrays with fewer than 2 elements
Returns correct area for arrays with all identical heights
Returns correct area for sorted arrays
Returns correct area for reverse sorted arrays
Code compiles and runs without errors
Implementation uses the two-pointer approach where the pointer with shorter height is moved inward
context_hints: Key insight: area is limited by the shorter line; moving the pointer at the shorter line inward may find a taller line that increases area
Start pointers at extremes (left=0, right=n-1) and converge inward
The optimal solution guarantees global maximum with this greedy approach because shorter lines can never contribute more to future areas

spec.objective: Implement the Container With Most Water algorithm that finds the maximum area between two vertical lines in an array, where area = min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i)
constraints: Must achieve O(n) time complexity with O(1) space complexity
Use two-pointer technique starting from both ends of the array
Must handle edge cases: empty array, single element, all equal heights, sorted arrays
acceptance_criteria: Returns correct maximum area for all valid inputs
Handles edge cases without errors (empty array returns 0, single element returns 0)
Correctly processes arrays of varying sizes from 0 to large n
Two-pointer logic: when heights are equal, either pointer can move; smaller height pointer always moves inward
target_files: solution.py (or solution.js/solution.java depending on expected language)
context_hints: Two-pointer approach: initialize left=0, right=n-1, compute area, then move the pointer with smaller height toward center
Reasoning: moving larger height pointer can only decrease or keep same min(height), while width also decreases, so area cannot increase
Alternative brute force O(n²) would check all pairs but is not optimal

spec.objective: Implement the Container With Most Water algorithm to find the maximum area between two vertical lines in an array
constraints: Must use efficient O(n) two-pointer technique, not brute force O(n²)
Return the maximum area as an integer
Handle edge cases: empty array, single element, all same heights
Input array can contain any non-negative integers
acceptance_criteria: Function accepts an array of integers and returns an integer
Correctly calculates area as min(height[i], height[j]) * (j-i)
Two-pointer approach: start from ends, move shorter height pointer inward
Returns correct maximum area for test cases: [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7] returns 49, [1,1] returns 1, [4,3,2,1,4] returns 16
context_hints: Two-pointer greedy technique: move the pointer at the shorter line because moving the taller line's pointer can never increase area (bounded by the shorter line)
Time complexity O(n), Space complexity O(1)
Python type hints recommended: def maxArea(heights: list[int]) -> int
